edges.description: Reduced ALG9 activity causes accumulation of incomplete DolPP-linked oligosaccharides.
Protein hypoglycosylation contributes to neurologic, hepatic, renal, cardiac, and skeletal manifestations.
ALG9-CDG glycoprotein dysfunction is associated with developmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, microcephaly, and brain MRI abnormalities.
Liveborn ALG9-CDG patients frequently show failure to thrive and hepatic or renal involvement.
The severe prenatal ALG9-CDG spectrum includes lethal skeletal dysplasia and ALG9 has been reported among CDG genes associated with nonimmune hydrops fetalis.
ALG9-CDG can include cardiovascular findings, particularly pericardial effusion in the reported infant case.
Developmental delay is a reported liveborn ALG9-CDG manifestation.
Hypotonia is a recurrent neurologic manifestation of ALG9-CDG.
Seizures are part of the liveborn ALG9-CDG neurologic spectrum.
Microcephaly is reported among liveborn ALG9-CDG patients.
Orphanet records progressive microcephaly as a frequent ALG9-CDG phenotype.
Intellectual disability is a very frequent neurodevelopmental manifestation in Orphanet's ALG9-CDG record.
Delayed myelination is a reported brain MRI finding in ALG9-CDG.
Cerebral and cerebellar volume loss are reported brain MRI findings.
Failure to thrive is a recurrent liveborn ALG9-CDG feature.
Hepatomegaly or hepatosplenomegaly is part of the visceral ALG9-CDG phenotype.
Renal cysts or cystic renal disease are recurrent ALG9-CDG findings.
Skeletal dysplasia is the defining severe prenatal ALG9-CDG phenotype.
ALG9 is reported among CDG genes associated with nonimmune hydrops fetalis.
Pericardial effusion is the reported cardiovascular manifestation.
Orphanet records abnormal heart morphology as a frequent cardiovascular phenotype in ALG9-CDG.
A type I carbohydrate-deficient transferrin pattern reports impaired transfer of complete N-glycan precursors to serum glycoproteins.
Accumulation of DolPP-GlcNAc2Man6 and DolPP-GlcNAc2Man8 reports the ALG9-dependent mannosyltransferase block in lipid-linked oligosaccharide assembly.

evidence.snippet: A homozygous point-mutation 1567G-->A (amino acid substitution E523K) was detected in the ALG9 gene.

All affected patients were shown to have a novel homozygous splice variant NM_024740.2: c.1173+2T>A in the ALG9 gene, encoding alpha-1,2-mannosyltransferase, involved in the formation of the lipid-linked oligosaccharide precursor of N-glycosylation.

nodes.description: Pathogenic ALG9 variants impair an alpha-1,2-mannosyltransferase required for lipid-linked oligosaccharide assembly in the endoplasmic reticulum.

evidence.snippet: Using this approach, we have found, in a patient with CDG, a deficiency of the ALG9 alpha 1,2 mannosyltransferase enzyme, which causes an accumulation of lipid-linked-GlcNAc(2)Man(6) and -GlcNAc(2)Man(8) structures, which was paralleled by the transfer of incomplete oligosaccharides precursors to protein.

nodes.description: Orphanet records abnormal heart morphology as a frequent cardiovascular manifestation of ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001627 | Abnormal heart morphology | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Delayed myelination has been documented on brain MRI in ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain showed volume loss in the cerebral hemispheres and cerebellum and delayed myelination.

Using this approach, we have found, in a patient with CDG, a deficiency of the ALG9 alpha 1,2 mannosyltransferase enzyme, which causes an accumulation of lipid-linked-GlcNAc(2)Man(6) and -GlcNAc(2)Man(8) structures, which was paralleled by the transfer of incomplete oligosaccharides precursors to protein.

readouts.interpretation: Accumulation of DolPP-GlcNAc2Man6 and DolPP-GlcNAc2Man8 reports the ALG9-dependent mannosyltransferase block in lipid-linked oligosaccharide assembly.

nodes.description: ALG9-CDG can include cardiovascular findings, including pericardial effusion reported in an affected infant.

evidence.snippet: The female infant's features included psychomotor retardation, seizures, hypotonia, diffuse brain atrophy with delayed myelination, failure to thrive, pericardial effusion, cystic renal disease, hepatosplenomegaly, esotropia, and inverted nipples.

nodes.description: Cardiac monitoring is prudent in ALG9-CDG because pericardial effusion is a reported disease manifestation and CDG expert recommendations support periodic cardiac surveillance for CDG patients.

evidence.snippet: Cardiac surveillance, including an echocardiogram and EKG, should be conducted at the time of diagnosis, annually throughout the first 5 years, followed by check-ups every 2-3 years if no concerns arise until adulthood.

nodes.description: Failure to thrive is reported in liveborn ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: Seven of these patients had a similar phenotype with failure to thrive, dysmorphic features, seizures, hepatic and/or renal cysts; the other three patients died in utero from a lethal skeletal dysplasia.

nodes.description: Cerebral and cerebellar volume loss has been reported in ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain showed volume loss in the cerebral hemispheres and cerebellum and delayed myelination.

nodes.description: Hypotonia is repeatedly reported in liveborn ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: The female infant's features included psychomotor retardation, seizures, hypotonia, diffuse brain atrophy with delayed myelination, failure to thrive, pericardial effusion, cystic renal disease, hepatosplenomegaly, esotropia, and inverted nipples.

nodes.description: Developmental delay and intellectual disability are recurrent neurologic manifestations of liveborn ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: The ALG9 defect found in the patient with CDG--who presented with developmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, and hepatomegaly--shows that efficient lipid-linked oligosaccharide synthesis is required for proper human development and physiology.
HP:0001263 | Global developmental delay | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: ALG9-CDG liveborn presentations include failure to thrive, hepatic enlargement or cysts, and renal cystic disease.

evidence.snippet: Seven of these patients had a similar phenotype with failure to thrive, dysmorphic features, seizures, hepatic and/or renal cysts; the other three patients died in utero from a lethal skeletal dysplasia.

The female infant's features included psychomotor retardation, seizures, hypotonia, diffuse brain atrophy with delayed myelination, failure to thrive, pericardial effusion, cystic renal disease, hepatosplenomegaly, esotropia, and inverted nipples.

nodes.description: Liver enlargement is part of the ALG9-CDG multisystem phenotype.

evidence.snippet: The ALG9 defect found in the patient with CDG--who presented with developmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, and hepatomegaly--shows that efficient lipid-linked oligosaccharide synthesis is required for proper human development and physiology.
HP:0002240 | Hepatomegaly | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Hydrops fetalis has been reported in ALG9-CDG within a Saudi CDG cohort.

evidence.snippet: The genes reported for CDG with NIHF for 15 distinct families include: PMM2 in 47% (7/15), ALG9 in 20% (3/15), ALG8 in 13% (2/15), ALG1 in 7% (1/15), MGAT2 in 7% (1/15), and COG6 7% (1/15).

nodes.description: Intellectual disability is a very frequent neurodevelopmental manifestation in Orphanet's ALG9-CDG record.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001249 | Intellectual disability | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Microcephaly is part of the liveborn ALG9-CDG neurologic phenotype.

evidence.snippet: All three suffered from intellectual disability, muscular hypotonia, microcephaly and renal cysts, but none had skeletal dysplasia.

nodes.description: Defective N-glycosylation manifests as neurodevelopmental disease, congenital anomalies, renal cystic disease, pericardial effusion, and severe skeletal dysplasia in the most severe fetal presentations.

evidence.snippet: To date, a total of 10 patients from 6 different families have been reported with one of four ALG9 mutations.
All three suffered from intellectual disability, muscular hypotonia, microcephaly and renal cysts, but none had skeletal dysplasia.

nodes.description: Liveborn ALG9-CDG includes neurodevelopmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, microcephaly, delayed myelination, and cerebral/cerebellar volume loss.

evidence.snippet: The ALG9 defect found in the patient with CDG--who presented with developmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, and hepatomegaly--shows that efficient lipid-linked oligosaccharide synthesis is required for proper human development and physiology.
Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain showed volume loss in the cerebral hemispheres and cerebellum and delayed myelination.

nodes.description: Pericardial effusion is a reported cardiovascular manifestation of ALG9-CDG.

evidence.snippet: The female infant's features included psychomotor retardation, seizures, hypotonia, diffuse brain atrophy with delayed myelination, failure to thrive, pericardial effusion, cystic renal disease, hepatosplenomegaly, esotropia, and inverted nipples.
HP:0001698 | Pericardial effusion | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Severe prenatal ALG9-CDG can present with lethal skeletal dysplasia with visceral malformations, and ALG9 has been reported among CDG genes causing nonimmune hydrops fetalis.

evidence.snippet: Our study shows that some pathogenic variants in ALG9 can present as a lethal skeletal dysplasia with visceral malformations as the most severe phenotype.
The genes reported for CDG with NIHF for 15 distinct families include: PMM2 in 47% (7/15), ALG9 in 20% (3/15), ALG8 in 13% (2/15), ALG1 in 7% (1/15), MGAT2 in 7% (1/15), and COG6 7% (1/15).

nodes.description: Orphanet records progressive microcephaly as a frequent ALG9-CDG manifestation.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000253 | Progressive microcephaly | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Renal cysts and cystic renal disease are recurrent ALG9-CDG findings.

evidence.snippet: The female infant's features included psychomotor retardation, seizures, hypotonia, diffuse brain atrophy with delayed myelination, failure to thrive, pericardial effusion, cystic renal disease, hepatosplenomegaly, esotropia, and inverted nipples.

nodes.description: Seizures occur in liveborn ALG9-CDG and can accompany failure to thrive and congenital anomalies.

evidence.snippet: She developed failure to thrive and seizures.
HP:0001250 | Seizure | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Some ALG9 variants cause a severe prenatal skeletal dysplasia with polycystic kidneys and multiple malformations.

evidence.snippet: A rare lethal autosomal recessive syndrome with skeletal dysplasia, polycystic kidneys and multiple malformations was first described by Gillessen-Kaesbach et al and subsequently by Nishimura et al.
HP:0002652 | Skeletal dysplasia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: No ALG9-specific disease-modifying therapy is established; seizure care is supportive and symptom-directed.

evidence.snippet: She developed failure to thrive and seizures.

nodes.description: Accumulated incomplete lipid-linked oligosaccharides are transferred to proteins, producing hypoglycosylation and a type I CDG biochemical pattern.

evidence.snippet: Hypoglycosylation was confirmed by the typical CDG type 1 pattern of serum transferrin analyzed by isoelectric focusing.

A defect in the ALG9 enzyme was suggested by the accumulation of the DolPP-GlcNAc2Man6 and DolPP-GlcNAc2Man8 in the patient's fibroblasts and confirmed by mutation analysis: the patient is homozygous for the ALG9 mutation p.Y286C.
Hypoglycosylation was confirmed by the typical CDG type 1 pattern of serum transferrin analyzed by isoelectric focusing.

readouts.interpretation: A type I carbohydrate-deficient transferrin pattern reports impaired transfer of complete N-glycan precursors to serum glycoproteins.
